prompt: What does it mean to "program to an interface, not an implementation"?
choices: Write all code inside a single user-interface file
Have callers depend on a contract of capabilities, while concrete vendors sit behind it
Avoid using any third-party vendors at all
Always choose the implementation with the fastest network response

prompt: Which of the following are direct benefits of making an implementation swappable behind a contract? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Callers can be tested with a fake implementation, with no network or database
A vendor can be replaced by writing one new adapter instead of editing every call site
The application automatically runs faster on every request
Teams can build against the contract before the real implementation exists

prompt: In the dependency-inversion principle, which way should the dependency arrows point?
choices: High-level business logic should depend directly on the chosen vendor's library
The vendor's library should depend on the business logic's internal classes
Both business logic and vendor code should depend on a shared abstraction
Each layer should depend on the layer physically above it in the file tree

prompt: A caller constructs a specific email vendor's client object inside its own code instead of receiving it. What problem does this create?
choices: It tightly couples the caller to that vendor, so swapping or faking it is no longer free
It makes the email arrive faster
It violates the rule that all code must live in one file
It has no downside as long as the vendor never changes its API
